Base directory for this skill: /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/skills/add

# Add Board Item

Create a new item in the `board/` project management system. The system spec is bundled with the plugin at `/Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/spec/board-system-spec.md`.

## Existing Board Items

Pre-loaded board data for duplicate detection:

filepath	type	status	priority	blocked_by	parent	title
./board/tasks/TASK-0002_deploy-landing-page.md	task	todo	-	-	NOTE-0002	Deploy landing page to Cloudflare Pages
./board/tasks/TASK-0003_nasa-data-insignia.md	task	todo	-	-	-	Apply for NASA Data Insignia
./board/tasks/TASK-0004_qa-cache-logging-deploy.md	task	todo	-	-	-	QA cache and logging before deploy
./board/planning/NOTE-0001_domain-strategy.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Domain strategy for nasapicture.com
./board/planning/NOTE-0002_landing-page.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Landing page for nasapicture.com
./board/planning/NOTE-0003_landing-page-critique.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Landing page design critique (gallery revision)
./board/planning/NOTE-0004_add-image-extension-routes-to-docs.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Add /image.jpg and /image-sd.jpg references to landing page and README
./board/planning/NOTE-0005_add-individual-metadata-routes.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Add individual metadata routes

Before creating, check if a similar item already exists. If a close match is found, ask the user if they still want to create a new one.

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

**Quick test:** "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → likely an EPIC.

If EPIC signals are present, suggest an EPIC + sub-TASKs with a concrete breakdown. For example:

> "This looks like it has multiple independent deliverables. Want me to create an EPIC with separate TASKs for each piece instead?"

**This is a soft suggestion only.** If the user says "just make it a task," comply without pushback.

This assessment only applies to TASK creation — do not second-guess EPICs, NOTEs, or other types.

## Arguments

The user provides the type and title: `/board:board-add task <title>`, `/board:board-add epic <title>`, etc.

If no type is given, ask the user what type of item to create.

## Determine Next ID

Run the ID assignment script:

```bash
bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/next-id.sh <TYPE> ./board
```

This scans all directories (including `board/archive/` and `.doc-ids`) and returns the next available 4-digit zero-padded ID.

## Create the File

Use the correct directory, frontmatter, and naming convention:

| Type | Directory | Filename Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| task | `board/tasks/` | `TASK-0014_add-batch-processing.md` |
| epic | `board/planning/` | `EPIC-0009_batch-mode.md` |
| adr | `board/planning/` | `ADR-0002_chose-webgpu-over-wasm.md` |
| note | `board/planning/` | `NOTE-0001_explore-wasm-option.md` |
| reference | `board/reference/` | `REFE-0007_webgpu-overview.md` |
| resource | `board/resources/` | `RESO-0001_webgpu-article.md` |

## Frontmatter Templates

Task:
```yaml
---
type: task
status: backlog
priority: 2
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Epic:
```yaml
---
type: epic
status: planning
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

ADR:
```yaml
---
type: adr
status: active
context_date: YYYY-MM-DD
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Note:
```yaml
---
type: note
status: inbox
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Reference:
```yaml
---
type: reference
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Resource (ask user for URL):
```yaml
---
type: resource
status: inbox
url: <url>
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

## Naming Rules

- Convert title to kebab-case for filename
- Task titles MUST be imperative mood (start with a verb). If the user gives a noun phrase, rewrite it (e.g., "batch processing" -> "Add batch processing support")
- Use today's date for `created`

## After Creation

Show the file path and ask if the user wants to add more detail to the body.


ARGUMENTS: NOTE add a "why is this needed" section that explains the APOD API. NOTE: add a "turn off the lights" icon that dims everything but the image for semi-isolated viewing

```md
# Add a "Why Is This Needed" Section That Explains the APOD API

Add a section to the landing page that explains what the APOD API is, why it exists, and why a proxy like nasapicture.com is useful. Help non-technical visitors understand the context — what NASA provides, the friction points (rate limits, video days, no stable image URL), and how this service solves them.

```

```md
# Add a "Turn Off the Lights" Icon for Semi-Isolated Image Viewing

Add a toggle icon (e.g., a lightbulb or moon) that dims everything on the landing page except the APOD hero image. Creates a semi-isolated viewing experience — lets visitors focus on the image without navigating away or opening it in a new tab. All surrounding UI (text, nav, background effects) fades to near-black, with a click/tap anywhere or the same icon to restore.

```

```md
---

# Fix Cloudflare beacon CORS error

Cross-Origin Request Blocked for `https://static.cloudflareinsights.com/beacon.min.js`. The Cloudflare Web Analytics script is failing to load due to CORS policy.

## Context

This is injected by Cloudflare (Web Analytics), not by project code. Likely caused by a Content-Security-Policy header or local dev environment mismatch. Investigate whether this reproduces in production or only locally, and whether CSP headers need adjusting or the analytics snippet needs updating in the Cloudflare dashboard.

```

```text
File created successfully at: /Users/grant/code/nasa-apod/board/tasks/TASK-0005_fix-cloudflare-beacon-cors-error.md
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:06:48Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
---
type: task
status: backlog
priority: 2
created: 2026-03-31
---

# Fix Cloudflare beacon SRI hash mismatch

The `integrity` attribute (sha512) on the Cloudflare Web Analytics beacon script does not match the content of the fetched resource. The computed hash is a zero-byte hash, suggesting the resource failed to load (likely related to the CORS error in TASK-0005) and the browser is comparing against an empty response.

## Context

This is a downstream symptom of the CORS failure — if TASK-0005 is resolved, this will likely resolve too. If not, the SRI hash in the injected snippet may be stale and need regeneration via the Cloudflare dashboard. Verify in production first.

```

```text
File created successfully at: /Users/grant/code/nasa-apod/board/tasks/TASK-0006_fix-cloudflare-beacon-sri-hash-mismatch.md
```
</details></tool-use>

- **TASK-0006** — Fix Cloudflare beacon SRI hash mismatch

Two issues from the W3C validator:

Both are minor. Want me to create tasks for these, or just fix them directly?

_**User (2026-04-01 01:11:11Z)**_

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:11:17Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:11:24Z)**_

Different default rulesets. The W3C Nu validator is the spec-conformance reference — it checks attribute *values* against the HTML spec (e.g., `datetime` must match a valid time format, not be empty). It also flags accessibility best practices like headings in sections.

`html-validate` is rule-based and only checks what's enabled in its config. By default it likely:

1. **Doesn't validate `datetime` value format** — it may check that the attribute is allowed on `<time>`, but not that the value conforms to the spec's date/time grammar
2. **Doesn't enforce headings in sections** — that's more of an accessibility/best-practice rule that needs to be explicitly enabled

You can check what rules are active:

```bash
npx html-validate --print-config /tmp/nasapicture.html
```

And there are rules like `valid-id` and element-specific content rules, but empty attribute values and section-heading requirements may need explicit opt-in. The W3C validator is stricter by default because it validates against the living HTML spec directly.

Want me to check the available config, or just move on to fixing the two issues?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:12:33Z)**_

Interesting — it actually has `attribute-allowed-values` enabled, which *should* catch the empty `datetime`. But you're right about the key distinction:

**html-validate checks static source HTML, not the rendered DOM.** The HTML it validated was the curl'd response from the server — which is the Worker's server-rendered output. If the `datetime=""` is present in that source, it should have flagged it. Let me check what's actually in the file.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:14:07Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:14:24Z)**_

No error — so `html-validate` doesn't consider an empty `datetime` invalid, even with `attribute-allowed-values` on. That rule likely checks for *wrong* values (e.g., `type="foo"` on an `<input>`), but treats empty strings as "no value provided" rather than "invalid value." The W3C validator is stricter — it validates the value against the spec's datetime grammar and rejects empty strings.

As for the missing section heading — there's no `require-heading-in-section` rule in the config at all.

So to summarize: **html-validate is a structural linter** (syntax, nesting, attribute correctness) while the **W3C validator is a spec-conformance checker** that validates attribute value formats and flags accessibility best practices. Both are useful, different coverage.

Want me to fix the two issues?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:17:34Z)**_

Short answer: **not fully.** There's a `html-validate:standard` preset described as "similar to the Nu Html Checker," but it still doesn't cover the two issues you hit:

1. **Empty `datetime` value** — there's no rule that validates the *format* of `datetime` attribute values. `attribute-allowed-values` checks enum-type attributes (like `type="text"` on inputs), not parsed formats like dates.
2. **Section requires heading** — no rule exists for this.

These are gaps in html-validate's rule set, not just config. The W3C Nu validator parses attribute values against the full HTML spec grammar, which html-validate doesn't attempt.

**Practical takeaway**: html-validate is good for fast local/CI linting (syntax, nesting, a11y basics), but it can't fully replace the W3C validator for spec conformance. You could use both — html-validate in CI for quick checks, and periodic W3C validation (via curl to the API like we just did) for deeper coverage.

Want me to fix the two issues, or create tasks for them?

Sources:
- [Configuration presets](https://html-validate.org/rules/presets.html)
- [Available rules](https://html-validate.org/rules/index.html)

```md
# Fix empty datetime attribute on time element

The `<time datetime="">` element in the landing page HTML (line 477) has an empty `datetime` attribute. The value is populated by JS after load, but the initial server-rendered HTML is invalid per the W3C validator — `datetime` must match a valid time-datetime format if present.

## Fix options

- Remove the `datetime` attribute from the initial HTML and only add it via JS when the value is available
- Pre-populate with a fallback date server-side

```

```md
# Fix hero section missing heading

The `.hero` `<section>` element lacks an `h2`–`h6` heading, flagged as a warning by the W3C validator. Sections should have identifying headings for document outline and accessibility.

## Fix options

- Add a visually-hidden heading (e.g., `<h2 class="sr-only">Today's Astronomy Picture</h2>`)
- Change the `<section>` to a `<div>` if it doesn't represent a distinct document section
```

_**User (2026-04-01 01:24:33Z)**_

how did you curl the W3C api?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:24:38Z)**_

I posted the HTML file to their API endpoint:

```bash
curl -s \
  -H "Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8" \
  --data-binary @/tmp/nasapicture.html \
  "https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json"
```

- `--data-binary @/tmp/nasapicture.html` sends the raw HTML as the request body
- `Content-Type: text/html` tells the validator it's receiving HTML (not a URL to check)
- `?out=json` returns structured JSON instead of an HTML results page

The Nu validator API accepts HTML directly via POST, so you don't need to host the page publicly or pass a URL. You could also pipe it inline:

```bash
curl -s https://nasapicture.com/ | curl -s \
  -H "Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8" \
  --data-binary @- \
  "https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json"
```

---

_**User (2026-04-01 01:27:40Z)**_

how would it be used with a non-HTML local page for local dev? like a react app?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:27:45Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:27:57Z)**_

A few approaches depending on what you want to validate:

### 1. Curl the local dev server
If it's running locally, just hit it the same way:
```bash
curl -s http://localhost:5173/ | curl -s \
  -H "Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8" \
  --data-binary @- \
  "https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json"
```
**Caveat**: This only validates the server-rendered/initial HTML — not the DOM after React hydrates and renders components. For an SPA, that initial HTML is mostly just a `<div id="root"></div>`.

### 2. Capture the rendered DOM from the browser
To validate what React actually renders, you need the live DOM:
```bash
# Using Playwright/Puppeteer to dump rendered HTML
npx playwright-cli eval \
  "const page = await browser.newPage(); \
   await page.goto('http://localhost:5173'); \
   console.log(await page.content());" \
  | curl -s -H "Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8" \
    --data-binary @- "https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json"
```

Or more practically, use the Chrome DevTools MCP or a test to serialize `document.documentElement.outerHTML` and pipe that to the validator.

### 3. html-validate has framework integrations
This is probably the most practical for CI/testing:
- **`html-validate-react`** — validates JSX/React component output
- **`html-validate/jest`** — use in tests: `expect(rendered).toHTMLValidate()`
- **`html-validate/vitest`** — same for Vitest

```js
// in a test file
import { render } from "@testing-library/react";

it("produces valid HTML", () => {
  const { container } = render(<MyComponent />);
  expect(container.innerHTML).toHTMLValidate();
});
```

This validates the rendered component output at test time — no browser or dev server needed.

### Bottom line

| Approach | Validates | Best for |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| Curl dev server → W3C API | Initial HTML only | SSR/static sites |
| Browser dump → W3C API | Full rendered DOM | SPAs, one-off checks |
| html-validate + test matcher | Component output | CI, per-component coverage |

For a React app, the test matcher approach is the most reliable since it catches issues per-component as part of your normal test suite.

---

_**User (2026-04-01 01:29:54Z)**_

your 3rd option is using html-validate, i'm talking about W3C validator

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:30:03Z)**_

Right — the W3C validator can only work with HTML strings, so for a client-rendered React app you need to capture the rendered DOM first. It comes down to:

### Curl the dev server + dump rendered DOM

```bash
# Puppeteer one-liner to get rendered HTML
node -e "
  const puppeteer = require('puppeteer');
  (async () => {
    const b = await puppeteer.launch();
    const p = await b.newPage();
    await p.goto('http://localhost:5173');
    console.log(await p.content());
    await b.close();
  })();
" | curl -s \
    -H "Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8" \
    --data-binary @- \
    "https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json"
```

### In a test

```js
import puppeteer from "puppeteer";

it("produces valid HTML", async () => {
  const browser = await puppeteer.launch();
  const page = await browser.newPage();
  await page.goto("http://localhost:5173");
  const html = await page.content();
  await browser.close();

  const res = await fetch("https://validator.w3.org/nu/?out=json", {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "text/html; charset=utf-8" },
    body: html,
  });
  const { messages } = await res.json();
  const errors = messages.filter((m) => m.type === "error");
  expect(errors).toEqual([]);
});
```

Either way the pattern is the same: **browser renders the DOM → serialize to HTML string → POST to the W3C API**. There's no way around the browser step for an SPA since the HTML only exists in memory after JS runs.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:36:57Z)**_

You're right — the test hits an external API (`validator.w3.org`) on every run, which is:

1. **Slow** — network round-trip in a test suite
2. **Flaky** — depends on W3C servers being up
3. **Rate-limited** — the public API isn't meant for CI hammering
4. **Not isolated** — tests shouldn't depend on external services

For a real test you'd want to either run the [Nu validator locally via Docker](https://github.com/validator/validator) or use html-validate's test matchers as the CI check and keep W3C validation as an occasional manual/scheduled audit. The snippet was more pseudocode than production-ready — my mistake.

_**User (2026-04-01 01:39:41Z)**_

i'm thinking of hosting the w3c validator in my homelab so we can use it while working on dev environments. what other validators/checkers/tests should we be running on dev stuff?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:39:47Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:39:59Z)**_

Good idea on self-hosting Nu. Here's what's worth running during dev, grouped by what they catch:

### Markup & Standards
- **W3C Nu validator** (self-hostable via Docker: `ghcr.io/validator/validator`) — HTML spec conformance
- **W3C CSS validator** (also self-hostable: `jigsaw`) — though Tailwind makes this less relevant

### Accessibility
- **axe-core** (`@axe-core/cli` or Playwright integration) — the industry standard, catches real WCAG violations not just warnings
- **pa11y** — CLI tool, good for quick one-off checks against rendered pages

### Performance
- **Lighthouse CI** (self-hostable via `lhci`) — performance, a11y, best practices, SEO in one pass. Can run headless against localhost
- **Unlighthouse** — runs Lighthouse across every page of a site automatically

### Security
- **Mozilla Observatory** (self-hostable) — checks security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, etc.)
- **OWASP ZAP** (Docker) — passive scan catches low-hanging security issues

### Links & SEO
- **linkinator** — finds broken links, works on localhost
- **html-validate** — you already have this, good for fast structural linting

### What I'd prioritize for your homelab

| Tool | Why | Self-host? |
|------|-----|------------|
| Nu validator | Spec conformance | Yes — Docker |
| Lighthouse CI | Performance + a11y + SEO in one shot | Yes — `lhci server` |
| axe-core | Best a11y coverage | No need — runs client-side |
| Observatory | Security headers | Yes — Docker |

These four cover most of what matters. Lighthouse alone handles a lot of overlap (it includes axe-core for a11y), so if you only set up two, I'd go **Nu + Lighthouse CI**.

Want me to create a reference on the board for this, or start looking into the self-hosting setup?
